title: Pest control including combined mating disruption and trapping
abstract: Disclosed is a new highly effective lure for the attraction of insect species, such as codling moth. The lure optionally includes pear ester kairomone (DA), dimethyl nonatriene, linalool oxide, and acetic acid. This lure is optionally used to reduce populations of female insects in combination with mating disruption to improve the effectiveness of mating disruption. This combination of insect control strategies has been shown to produce unexpected synergistic benefits.
claims: Claims CLAIMS What is claimed is: 1. A lure configured to attract female codling moth, the lure comprising: optionally codling moth pheromone; pear ester kairomone; dimethyl nonatriene, wherein the pear ester kairomone and dimethyl nonatriene are disposed in a solid emitter; linalool oxide; and acetic acid. 2. The lure of claim 1, wherein the linalool oxide is in the pyranoid form. 3. The lure of claim 1 or 2, wherein the pear ester kairomone, dimethyl nonatriene and linalool oxide are disposed in a solid emitter and acetic acid is disposed in a liquid dispenser. 4. The lure of claim 3, wherein a shape and matrix of the solid emitter is used to control release rate of the pear ester. 5. The lure of claim 1-3 or 4, wherein the lure is configured to preferentially attract female codling moth relative to male codling moth, at a rate of at least 2:1. 6. The lure of claim 1-4 or 5, further comprising any of the compounds listed in Table 1 or Table 2. 7. The lure of claim 1-5 or 6, further comprising a killing agent configured to kill codling moth, the killing agent optionally including an insecticide. 8. A trap configured to capture female codling moth and comprising the lure of claim 1-6 or 7. 9. The trap of claim 8, further comprising a mating disruption compound configured to disrupt matting of codling moth. 10. The trap of claim 9, wherein a source of the mating disruption compound is disposed between 5 and 100 feet from the trap. 11. The trap of claim 9 or 10, wherein the mating disruption compound includes at least one of CM pheromone and pear ester (DA). 12. The trap of claim 9-10 or 11, wherein the trap is configured to increase effectiveness of the mating disruption compound at disrupting codling moth mating exponentially as a function of a number of female codling moths trapped. 13. The trap of claim 9-10 or 11, wherein the trap is configured to increase effectiveness of the mating disruption compound at disrupting codling moth mating non-linearly as a function of a number of female codling moths trapped. 14. The trap of claim 9-12 or 13, wherein the trap in combination with the mating disruption compound results in a synergistic effect that reduces codling moth population by a factor greater than the sum of separate female trapping females and mating disruption. 15. The lure of claim 1-13 or 14, further comprising an attraction inhibitor configured to inhibit attraction of male codling moth and increase selectivity of female codling moth attraction by the lure. 16. A method of managing an insect infestation, the method comprising: placing a trap and lure in an area of the infestation, the lure being configured to attract females of the insects and thus reduce the density of females within the infestation by trapping at least a fraction of the females of the insect infestation; and providing a mating disruption compound in the area of the infestation, the mating disruption compound being configured to prevent males of the insects from mating with females of the insects in the infestation area that are not part of the trapped fraction. 17. The method of claim 16, further comprising: determining or predicting an abundance of the insect (female) infestation; determining or predicting that the abundance is or will be above a predetermined threshold, wherein the step of placing the trap and lure in the area of infestation is responsive to this determination or prediction. 18. The method of claim 17, wherein the predicting is dependent on an infestation history and temperature measurements. 19. The method of claim 17 or 18, wherein the predicting is dependent on a number of male insects trapped. 20. The method of claim 16-18 or 19, wherein the trap in combination with the mating disruption compound results in a synergistic effect that reduces codling moth population by a factor greater than the sum of separate female trapping females and mating disruption. 21. The method of claim 16-19 or 20, wherein the trap increases effectiveness of the mating disruption compound at disrupting codling moth mating non-linearly or exponentially as a function of a number of female codling moths trapped, relative to mating disruption without the trap. 22. The method of claim 16-20 or 21, wherein the trap and lure are placed in the area of infestation prior to providing the mating disruption compound in the area of infestation. 23. The method of claim 16-21 or 22, wherein the trap and lure are placed between 10 and 200 feet from a source of the mating disruption compound. 24. The method of claim 16-22 or 23, wherein the trap and lure include the trap or lure of claim 1-14 or 15. 25. The method of claim 16-23 or 24, wherein the insect infestation is an infestation of codling moth, European grape vine moth (EGVM), Lobesia botrana in grape (vines), navel orangeworm (NOW), Amyelois transitella, Noctuidae, fall armyworm (FAW), or Spodoptera frugiperda, 26. The system or method of claim 1-24 or 25, wherein the pear ester kairomone, linalool oxide and dimethyl nonatriene are evenly distributed throughout the a/the solid emitter by compounding with monomers of a plastic of the solid emitter prior to addition to an extrusion device.
